locations.raw_source_name: Jacobs, T, Jacobson, S R, Fortea, J, Berger, J S, Vedvyas, A, Marsh, K, He, T, Gutierrez-Jimenez, E, Fillmore, N R, Gonzalez, M, Figueredo, L, Gaggi, N L, Plaska, C R, Pomara, N, Blessing, E, Betensky, R, Rusinek, H, Zetterberg, H, Blennow, K, Glodzik, L, Wisniweski, T M, de Leon, M J, Osorio, R S & Ramos-Cejudo, J 2024, 'The neutrophil to lymphocyte ratio associates with markers of Alzheimer’s disease pathology in cognitively unimpaired elderly people', Immunity & ageing , vol. 21, no. 1, 32. https://doi.org/10.1186/s12979-024-00435-2

crossref.publisher: Springer Science and Business Media LLC
crossref.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title><jats:sec>
                <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                <jats:p>An elevated neutrophil–lymphocyte ratio (NLR) in blood has been associated with Alzheimer’s disease (AD). However, an elevated NLR has also been implicated in many other conditions that are risk factors for AD, prompting investigation into whether the NLR is directly linked with AD pathology or a result of underlying comorbidities. Herein, we explored the relationship between the NLR and AD biomarkers in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) of cognitively unimpaired (CU) subjects. Adjusting for sociodemographics, APOE4, and common comorbidities, we investigated these associations in two cohorts: the Alzheimer’s Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI) and the M.J. de Leon CSF repository at NYU. Specifically, we examined associations between the NLR and cross-sectional measures of amyloid-β42 (Aβ42), total tau (t-tau), and phosphorylated tau<jats:sub>181</jats:sub> (p-tau), as well as the trajectories of these CSF measures obtained longitudinally.</jats:p>
              </jats:sec><jats:sec>
                <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                <jats:p>A total of 111 ADNI and 190 NYU participants classified as CU with available NLR, CSF, and covariate data were included. Compared to NYU, ADNI participants were older (73.79 vs. 61.53, <jats:italic>p</jats:italic> < 0.001), had a higher proportion of males (49.5% vs. 36.8%, <jats:italic>p</jats:italic> = 0.042), higher BMIs (27.94 vs. 25.79, <jats:italic>p</jats:italic> < 0.001), higher prevalence of hypertensive history (47.7% vs. 16.3%, <jats:italic>p</jats:italic> < 0.001), and a greater percentage of Aβ-positivity (34.2% vs. 20.0%, <jats:italic>p</jats:italic> = 0.009). In the ADNI cohort, we found cross-sectional associations between the NLR and CSF Aβ42 (β = -12.193, <jats:italic>p</jats:italic> = 0.021), but not t-tau or p-tau. In the NYU cohort, we found cross-sectional associations between the NLR and CSF t-tau (β = 26.812, <jats:italic>p</jats:italic> = 0.019) and p-tau (β = 3.441, <jats:italic>p</jats:italic> = 0.015), but not Aβ42. In the NYU cohort alone, subjects classified as Aβ + (<jats:italic>n</jats:italic> = 38) displayed a stronger association between the NLR and t-tau (β = 100.476, <jats:italic>p</jats:italic> = 0.037) compared to Aβ- subjects or the non-stratified cohort. In both cohorts, the same associations observed in the cross-sectional analyses were observed after incorporating longitudinal CSF data.</jats:p>
              </jats:sec><jats:sec>
                <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                <jats:p>We report associations between the NLR and Aβ42 in the older ADNI cohort, and between the NLR and t-tau and p-tau in the younger NYU cohort. Associations persisted after adjusting for comorbidities, suggesting a direct link between the NLR and AD. However, changes in associations between the NLR and specific AD biomarkers may occur as part of immunosenescence.</jats:p>
              </jats:sec>
title: The neutrophil to lymphocyte ratio associates with markers of Alzheimer’s disease pathology in cognitively unimpaired elderly people

assertion.value: Each site involved in data collection for the ADNI received local Institutional Review Board (IRB) approval. Written informed consent was obtained from each enrolled participant. All NYU studies involved in the collection of data used in this study were approved by the NYU Grossman School of Medicine Institutional Review Board (IRB). Written informed consent was obtained from each participant.
